critic_raw: The transformer-based ticket escalation prediction experiment has several areas to probe. First, the escalation label is defined as 'any ticket routed to or reassigned to senior/tier-2 support queue' — but this operational definition is subject to systematic differences between agents. Some agents may escalate conservatively (only for genuinely difficult tickets) while others escalate liberally. The model may learn agent escalation behavior patterns rather than ticket content escalation risk. Second, agent names and IDs are excluded and replaced with aggregate statistics, but these aggregate statistics (agent tenure, prior escalation rate) are computed on the training set. In the test period (months 31-36), new agents will have joined and experienced agents will have different tenure levels — the aggregate statistics may not accurately represent the test distribution. Third, the product-line stratification is applied 'within each fold' — but if a product line launches during months 31-36 that didn't exist in months 1-24, its tickets will have no product-line representation in training.
defender_raw: Each concern is addressed. On agent behavior confounding: this is acknowledged — the design specifically excludes agent IDs and uses aggregate statistics to prevent memorizing agent-specific biases. The experiment tests whether ticket content (sequence model) predicts escalation better than metadata + BoW (baseline). If agent behavior is the primary driver, both models would perform similarly and the experiment would correctly find no advantage for sequence modeling. On aggregate statistics for new agents: agent tenure and prior escalation rate in the test period are computed using the same training-set standardization — new agents joining in months 31-36 would have zero or unknown prior escalation rates, which the model handles via the defined preprocessing (new agents get missing-value imputation from training). This is a deployment reality and both models handle it equally. On new product lines: if a new product line launches in months 31-36, those tickets would either be labeled 'unknown' or mapped to 'other' in the product-line feature — the design handles unseen categorical values via standard preprocessing. The product-line stratification for evaluation would show lower performance on new product lines, which is an appropriate and honest result.
adjudication_raw: Three concerns raised: agent behavior confounding, aggregate statistics for new agents, and new product lines in test. All three are addressed: agent behavior drives equal performance for both models (not a flaw, honest evaluation); new agents handled by missing-value imputation; new product lines handled by 'other' mapping with stratified analysis revealing any issues. These are standard deployment realities, not methodological flaws. Verdict: defense_wins.
